sections.title: Why we start here
sections.body:
Before goals, before grades, before the grind — there is you. KP God First is about anchoring to something bigger than the next deadline. Whether your anchor is faith, family, a deeper sense of purpose, or simply your own values, you need one. Discipline without a center collapses under pressure. With a center, pressure becomes fuel.

In this module you'll name your anchor and commit to checking in with it daily. This is not religious instruction — it is mental architecture.

sections.title: Name your anchor
sections.prompt: What is the one thing — bigger than school, bigger than career — that you want to live in alignment with? Write it out. Be specific.

sections.title: Daily centering practice
sections.prompt: Take 60 seconds today to sit with your anchor. Note one word for how you feel right now.

modules.title: Know Your Why
modules.subtitle: Define the motivation behind the work

sections.title: The cost of working without a why
sections.body:
Most students burn out not because the work is too hard but because they forgot why they signed up. Your why is the answer you give yourself at 2 a.m. when you'd rather quit. If it's borrowed from your parents, your friends, or a LinkedIn post — it won't hold.

A real why is specific, personal, and a little embarrassing to say out loud. That's how you know it's yours.

sections.title: The five whys
sections.prompt: Write down what you're working toward right now. Then ask 'why?' five times in a row, each answer building on the last. The fifth answer is your real why.

questions.prompt: A strong personal 'why' is most useful when:
Which of these is a sign your 'why' is borrowed, not yours?
options: You can defend it to a stranger
You learned it from a podcast yesterday

questions.prompt: Why does the module recommend five 'whys' instead of one?
options: It surfaces the real motivation under surface answers

modules.title: Ball Is Life
modules.subtitle: Accountability and teamwork lessons from the court

sections.title: Box out, every play
sections.body:
Basketball teaches the same lesson every possession: you are accountable for your assignment whether or not anyone is watching. Box out when the ball is across the court. Talk on defense even when you're tired. Show up to a workout your coach didn't schedule.

In life, no one is watching most of the time. That's where discipline is built — or quietly lost.

sections.title: Your assignment
sections.prompt: What is one 'assignment' in your life right now that no one is checking on, but that you know matters? How will you box out this week?

sections.title: Permission to imagine
sections.body:
What if you actually pulled it off? What if the company you're afraid to start became real? What if you applied to the program you assumed you wouldn't get into?

'What if' is the only sentence that can move you out of the version of your life that's already happening. The risk is not in asking it — the risk is in never asking it and finding out at 35 that you spent a decade defending a default.

sections.title: Three what-ifs
sections.prompt: Write three 'what if' statements about your life that you've never said out loud. They should feel slightly uncomfortable to type.

sections.title: One bold move daily
sections.prompt: Each day, name one small action that moves you toward a 'what if'. Send the email. Make the ask. Open the doc.

modules.title: LinkedIn
modules.subtitle: Professional branding and networking that actually compounds

sections.title: Your profile is a search result
sections.body:
Recruiters search for you before they meet you. Your profile is not a resume — it's a search result. The headline matters more than the work experience. The photo matters more than the headline. And consistency over four years matters more than any single post.

This module ends with you making one real change to your profile today.

sections.title: The three-line headline
sections.body: A great student headline does three things in under 220 characters: (1) names what you are, (2) names what you're building toward, (3) gives one specific signal. Example: 'CS @ Howard | Building consumer apps with real users | Prev: SWE intern @ Capital One'. Specific beats clever every time.

sections.title: Update your headline now
sections.body: Open your LinkedIn profile and rewrite your headline in the three-line format. Come back and confirm when done.

sections.title: Streaks > sprints
sections.body:
Discipline is not a personality trait — it is a default. The job is to lower the activation energy of the things you want to do daily and raise it for the things you don't.

The goals you create in LOCKD In are the lab. Make them small enough that you can't honestly skip. A mile a day. Ten pages. One application. The compound interest is real.

sections.title: Pick your one
sections.prompt: What is ONE habit you will commit to every day for the next 30 days? Write it as a goal you can check off in this app.

sections.title: The cost of the mask
sections.body:
Performing a version of yourself you think people want is exhausting and, eventually, illegible. People connect with specificity, not polish. The story of where you're actually from, what you actually love, what you've actually struggled with — that is your edge.

In a feed full of curated wins, the realest profile wins.

sections.title: What you usually edit out
sections.prompt: What is one true thing about your story that you usually edit out when you introduce yourself? Why?

sections.title: Discipline is a team sport
sections.body:
The lone wolf myth is a marketing campaign. Every person you admire has a group chat. They have people they show their work to before it's ready, people who notice when they go quiet, people who hold the line on standards when motivation drops.

Join one org in LOCKD In this week. Make your goals public to that group. Discipline you outsource to a community is discipline that survives.

sections.title: Join a community
sections.body: Browse student orgs and join at least one. Make at least one of your goals public so the community can see your check-ins.

sections.title: Standards, not perfectionism
sections.body:
Perfectionism is the enemy of shipping. High standards are the enemy of mediocrity. The difference: perfectionism is about avoiding the judgment of others; high standards are about respecting the people your work is for.

Ship the project. Then make the next one better. That is the loop.

sections.title: The one detail
sections.prompt: What is one detail in your current work — a project, an application, a profile — that you've been letting slide that is below your real standard? Fix it today.

sections.title: From why to what
sections.body:
Your 'why' is your motivation. Your purpose is the shape your life takes because of it. A purpose statement is one sentence you can recite that tells you, for the next ten years, what to say yes to and what to say no to.

This is the last module. After you finish it, you are LOCKD In.

sections.title: Your one-sentence purpose
sections.prompt: Draft a single sentence: 'My purpose is to ___ so that ___.' Don't worry about getting it perfect — you'll edit it for years. Just write the first real version.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a 'why' and a 'purpose' in this curriculum?
options: Why is motivation; purpose is the shape it gives your life
Why is for school; purpose is for work

questions.prompt: What are you supposed to do with your purpose statement after writing it?
options: Use it to decide what to say yes and no to
